name: Influenza (Flu)
overview: Influenza is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. It typically presents with sudden fever, cough, fatigue, and body aches, and can range from mild to severe.
underlying_cause: Infection with influenza A or B viruses, usually spread through respiratory droplets from coughs, sneezes, or contaminated surfaces.
first_line_treatment: Rest and adequate hydration to support recovery
Antiviral medications may be considered early in high-risk patients when prescribed by a clinician
Fever and pain management with over-the-counter agents as directed by a healthcare provider
management_strategies: Isolation during the contagious period to reduce transmission
Monitoring for complications such as pneumonia or dehydration
Early clinical evaluation for high-risk groups (elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised, chronic lung/heart disease)
supportive_care: Increased fluid intake
Rest in a comfortable environment
Humidified air for cough and congestion relief
Nutritious, easy-to-digest meals

medication_categories.purpose: May reduce duration and severity when started early under medical supervision

medication_categories.note: Use only as directed on label or by a clinician
Not a substitute for medical evaluation if breathing worsens

home_care: Stay home until fever-free for 24 hours without fever-reducing medication
Use tissues and hand hygiene to protect household contacts
Avoid close contact with vulnerable individuals
Track temperature and symptom progression daily
diagnostic_tests: Clinical assessment when testing is unavailable
recovery_timeline: Most healthy adults recover within 1–2 weeks. Fatigue and cough may persist longer. Complications can prolong recovery.
prevention: Avoiding contact with sick individuals during peak season
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath at rest
Persistent chest pain or pressure
Confusion, inability to wake, or severe dizziness
High fever not responding to appropriate measures
Symptoms that improve then return with high fever and worse cough
patient_education: Influenza is not the same as the common cold and can cause serious complications
Antibiotics do not treat viral influenza
Vaccination remains the most effective preventive strategy
Seek urgent care if warning signs develop

name: COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2 infection)
overview: COVID-19 is a respiratory and systemic illness caused by SARS-CoV-2. Presentations range from mild upper respiratory symptoms to severe pneumonia and multi-organ involvement.
underlying_cause: Infection with SARS-CoV-2, primarily transmitted via respiratory droplets and aerosols in close contact settings.
first_line_treatment: Supportive care for mild illness: rest, hydration, and symptom monitoring
Clinical evaluation to determine eligibility for antiviral or monoclonal therapies in high-risk patients
Oxygen support and hospital care for moderate-to-severe disease
management_strategies: Risk stratification based on age, comorbidities, and oxygen saturation
Isolation per public health guidance to prevent transmission
Monitoring for progression: worsening breathlessness, hypoxia, or persistent high fever
supportive_care: Adequate hydration and nutrition
Rest and gradual return to activity
Pulse oximetry monitoring when advised by a clinician
Throat soothing measures for cough and sore throat

medication_categories.purpose: May reduce severe outcomes in eligible patients when prescribed early
medication_categories.note: Requires medical assessment and eligibility criteria

medication_categories.purpose: May be used for wheeze or underlying lung disease

home_care: Self-isolate according to local guidelines
Monitor temperature, breathing, and oxygen levels if available
Inform close contacts per public health recommendations
Avoid sharing personal items and maintain ventilation
diagnostic_tests: Chest imaging when pneumonia is suspected
recovery_timeline: Mild cases often improve within 1–2 weeks. Some patients experience prolonged fatigue or post-viral symptoms lasting weeks to months.
prevention: Staying up to date with recommended vaccinations
Hand hygiene and masking during high transmission periods when advised
warning_signs: Severe difficulty breathing or blue lips/face
Persistent pain or pressure in the chest
New confusion or inability to stay awake
SpO2 persistently below clinician-recommended thresholds
Inability to keep fluids down or signs of dehydration
patient_education: Not all cases require hospitalization; many resolve with supportive care
Long COVID symptoms can occur and warrant medical follow-up
Vaccination reduces risk of severe disease
Do not use unproven therapies without medical guidance

name: Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)
overview: Community-acquired pneumonia is an infection of the lung parenchyma acquired outside the hospital. It commonly causes fever, productive cough, chest pain, and shortness of breath.
underlying_cause: Bacterial pathogens (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae), viruses, or atypical organisms infecting the lower respiratory tract.
first_line_treatment: Empiric antibiotic therapy when bacterial infection is suspected, selected by a clinician based on severity and local guidelines
Hospital admission for moderate-to-severe cases requiring oxygen or IV therapy
Supportive care including oxygen, fluids, and respiratory support as needed
management_strategies: Severity assessment tools (e.g., CURB-65) to guide outpatient vs inpatient care
Blood cultures and sputum studies when feasible before antibiotics in severe disease
Follow-up imaging if symptoms fail to improve within expected timeframe
supportive_care: Supplemental oxygen to maintain adequate saturation
IV or oral hydration for dehydration
Chest physiotherapy when indicated
Nutritional support during prolonged illness

medication_categories.purpose: Treat bacterial and atypical causes when clinically indicated
medication_categories.note: Selection and duration must be determined by a licensed clinician
Not first-line for all pneumonia cases

home_care: Complete prescribed antibiotic course if outpatient therapy is deemed appropriate
Rest and increased fluid intake
Monitor breathing rate and oxygen symptoms closely
Return for reassessment if no improvement in 48–72 hours
diagnostic_tests: Complete blood count and inflammatory markers
recovery_timeline: Improvement often begins within 48–72 hours of appropriate therapy, with full recovery over 2–6 weeks depending on severity and host factors.
prevention: Pneumococcal vaccination per age and risk-based schedules
Smoking cessation and management of chronic lung disease
warning_signs: Severe shortness of breath or inability to speak full sentences
Cyanosis or oxygen saturation below safe thresholds
Altered mental status or hypotension
High fever with rigors not improving with treatment
Significant pleuritic chest pain suggesting complications
patient_education: Pneumonia can be life-threatening and requires professional assessment
Not all cough with fever is pneumonia — imaging often confirms diagnosis
Early treatment improves outcomes
Never share or stop antibiotics without medical advice

name: Acute Bronchitis
overview: Acute bronchitis is a short-term inflammation of the bronchi, usually following a viral upper respiratory infection. It presents with cough, with or without sputum, often lasting 1–3 weeks.
underlying_cause: Most cases are viral (e.g., influenza, rhinovirus, adenovirus). Bacterial causes are uncommon and should not be assumed without clinical evaluation.
first_line_treatment: Supportive care as the mainstay for viral bronchitis
Inhaled bronchodilators may be considered for wheeze under medical guidance
Antibiotics are generally not indicated unless a bacterial cause is suspected by a clinician
management_strategies: Differentiate from pneumonia and asthma exacerbation
Reassess if cough persists beyond 3 weeks or symptoms worsen
supportive_care: Hydration and humidified air
Rest
Honey or warm fluids for cough soothing in appropriate age groups
Avoid smoke and respiratory irritants

medication_categories.note: Limited evidence; use cautiously, especially in children per clinician advice

home_care: Avoid smoking and secondhand smoke exposure
Use a humidifier if air is dry
Track cough duration and any new fever or breathlessness
Maintain activity as tolerated
diagnostic_tests: Chest X-ray if pneumonia is suspected
Pulse oximetry if breathlessness is present
recovery_timeline: Cough commonly lasts 1–3 weeks even as other symptoms resolve. Complete recovery is expected in uncomplicated viral cases.
prevention: Hand hygiene and avoiding sick contacts
warning_signs: High fever lasting more than 3 days
Significant shortness of breath or wheezing not responding to usual measures
Cough with blood-streaked sputum
Chest pain suggesting alternative diagnosis
Symptoms lasting more than 3 weeks
patient_education: Antibiotics are usually not needed for acute viral bronchitis
Cough may persist after infection clears — this is common
Return for evaluation if breathing worsens
Distinguishing bronchitis from pneumonia requires clinical assessment

name: Migraine
overview: Migraine is a recurrent neurological disorder characterized by episodic headache, often unilateral, pulsating, and associated with nausea, photophobia, or phonophobia.
underlying_cause: Complex neurovascular and genetic factors involving trigeminovascular activation, cortical spreading depression, and altered sensory processing.
first_line_treatment: Acute therapy with clinician-selected analgesics or migraine-specific agents when diagnosed
Rest in a dark, quiet environment during attacks
Preventive therapy for frequent or disabling migraines under specialist care
management_strategies: Headache diary to identify triggers and response to therapy
Lifestyle measures: sleep regularity, hydration, stress management
Neurology referral for refractory or atypical presentations
supportive_care: Cold compress to head or neck
Caffeine in moderation may help some patients when advised
Relaxation techniques and biofeedback

medication_categories.note: Avoid medication-overuse headache with excessive use
Often combined with acute migraine therapy

home_care: Identify and avoid personal triggers (sleep loss, certain foods, stress)
Maintain consistent sleep-wake schedule
Limit screen time during attacks
diagnostic_tests: Clinical diagnosis based on history and neurological exam
Neuroimaging when red flags or atypical features are present
recovery_timeline: Individual attacks typically last 4–72 hours if untreated. Preventive strategies can reduce frequency over weeks to months.
prevention: Preventive medications when recommended by a neurologist
warning_signs: Sudden worst headache of life ('thunderclap')
New neurological deficits, confusion, or seizure
Headache with fever, stiff neck, or rash
Headache after head trauma
New headache after age 50 or in pregnancy
patient_education: Migraine is a neurological condition, not simply a stress headache
Overuse of acute medications can worsen headache frequency
Red-flag symptoms require emergency evaluation

name: Angina Pectoris
overview: Angina pectoris is chest discomfort caused by myocardial ischemia, often due to coronary artery disease. It may present as pressure, squeezing, or pain triggered by exertion or stress.
underlying_cause: Imbalance between myocardial oxygen demand and supply, commonly from atherosclerotic coronary narrowing or spasm.
first_line_treatment: Immediate medical evaluation for any new or worsening chest pain
Nitrate therapy and rest as directed by emergency or cardiology teams
Long-term anti-anginal and cardiovascular risk reduction under cardiology care
management_strategies: ECG and cardiac biomarkers in acute presentations
Risk factor modification: blood pressure, lipids, diabetes, smoking cessation
Revascularization when indicated by coronary anatomy and symptoms
supportive_care: Stop exertion at onset of symptoms
Stress reduction techniques as adjunct to medical therapy
Cardiac rehabilitation after confirmed coronary disease

medication_categories.purpose: Acute and preventive relief of angina

home_care: Know personal angina action plan from cardiologist
Avoid heavy exertion until evaluated
Never ignore chest pain lasting more than a few minutes
diagnostic_tests: Exercise stress testing or imaging stress tests
recovery_timeline: Acute episodes may resolve within minutes with rest and appropriate therapy. Long-term outlook depends on coronary disease severity and treatment.
prevention: Regular physical activity as approved by cardiology
warning_signs: Chest pain at rest or lasting >15 minutes
Pain radiating to arm, jaw, or back with sweating or nausea
Syncope or severe shortness of breath with chest pain
Sudden increase in angina frequency or severity
patient_education: Chest pain may indicate a heart attack — call emergency services when severe or persistent
This educational summary is not a substitute for urgent cardiac assessment
Risk factors should be managed lifelong
